data.title: Vornado Takes A Bite Out Of The Big Apple, As Demand For Space Returns
data.description:
A Key NYC REIT Filling The Demand For Space, But Struggling With Margins

With its most recent earnings results on May 5th still fresh and having beat estimates by 12 cents, Vornado Realty Trust (NYSE:VNO) will be the focus of my article today as I take a look at the niche of office REITs.

To get a sense of what this REIT has been up to lately, according to its company website and presentation, it has been active in developing the Penn District of Manhattan, to the tune of millions of square feet in the heart of New York City:

After my research today into 6 different categories, I determined to take a neutral approach and call this stock a Hold.

The following table shows how I got to my score of 6.57 (on a scale of 1 to 10), and the factors that contributed:

I gave greater weight to top-line factors and sector/macro factors I thought will drive future upside, and that is portfolio/pipeline growth and market demand for office and retail in NYC. My future price target also expects a 3-year positive upside.

Where there was weakness was in the profit margin, which can hurt earnings growth, an elevated debt/equity ratio, and disappointing dividend growth trends so far.

Favorable Macro Factors Such as Demand For Office And Retail in NYC, And New Population

In this section, I will focus on the sector and macro environment that impacts this REIT.

Its niche is office and retail as opposed to medical or mortgage REITs, and its "portfolio is concentrated in the nation’s key market — New York City — along with premier assets in both Chicago and San Francisco," according to its corporate website.

So, naturally, I think we should consider what kind of demand there will be for office and retail in those urban markets, particularly NYC.

As someone who worked remote during the 2020 pandemic and afterwards, I still remember the dozens of empty/closed office buildings, not to mention the various stores at the mall that had to close or go out of business completely.

Since then, however, there is a changing trend. In a January article, CNBC pointed to this change:

Demand for New York City office space has finally returned to pre-pandemic levels, driven by an influx of new workers as well as a drive by employers to see current workers return to the office.

Further, in late December, the New York Post also highlighted this trend:

It’s happening all over Midtown. Available floors quickly are gobbled up before data-trackers even know about it, not only at newly constructed “trophy” towers but at sound older locations as well.

I think demand for retail space also is notable, with a January note from real estate firm CBRE Group (CBRE) saying that avg. asking rent in Manhattan's retail corridors is "up 4% year-over-year."

There also is the role of population growth, I think, and how it can impact demand for offices and retail.

From an official City Of New York press release this March, Vornado's primary market of NYC has seen both an influx of people as well as fewer vacant retail properties than in pandemic times:

A recent report found that the city leads the nation in attracting young talent with nearly 500,000 recent college graduates choosing to live in the five boroughs since 2021. The updated census estimate also comes as the city has seen five straight quarters with fewer empty storefronts. The citywide storefront vacancy rate is down to 11 percent, from 30 percent in 2020.

Based on the above evidence, such as recent growth in demand for office and retail space, and an influx of new people, my opinion is that the macro environment looks to be an upside factor for Vornado. It is also evidence that pandemics and remote work do not entirely eliminate the need for offices and stores, but rather just put them on pause for a while.

For this article, I picked just two comparable stocks within the same sector, since I wanted to focus on REITs that have a lot of exposure to NYC and those are: SL Green Realty Corp. (SLG) and Empire State Realty Trust (ESRT).

Positive View Of Top-Line Growth Potential Comes From Portfolio Pipeline, And Tenant List

Next, I will discuss some factors impacting top-line growth potential, with an eye towards the future growth.

Considering the existing nature of Vornado's portfolio, according to its quarterly financial supplement, it is leasing to some big corporate names yet no single tenant in its top 10 makes up more than 8% of annualized rent, something I think reduces the downside risk of overconcentration in a single tenant:

So, one growth factor could be if the above companies do well then they are more likely to keep leasing and paying higher rents. The good news is, its #1 tenant Meta Platforms (META), the Facebook parent company, just "reported stronger-than-expected revenue in the first quarter and provided second-quarter guidance that was in line with Wall Street’s expectations," according to CNBC.

Also on its tenant list is Amazon (AMZN), who recently reported a YoY growth in both net sales and operating income for Q1, according to the company's release.

Another growth factor is growth in the portfolio through acquisitions, and a development pipeline, since I believe a growing portfolio can drive future rental income.

Earlier I showed in the visual that the Penn District is a major development pipeline, with Penn15 and its +2MM sq. ft of leasable space a factor that can positively drive future rental income.

However, one segment I see potential in is need for parking, and I expect some future upside to come from owning parking garages. Vornado in its recent presentation actually showed that its portfolio includes a few thousand sq. ft of parking garages diversified across NYC, Rosslyn (Virginia), and San Francisco (the Mart):

Besides office and parking, from its portfolio we can learn that Vornado has been also developing or has developed space for "70+ new food and beverage offerings," which I think is another potential growth driver beyond just office:

This ties into a personal experience when working once in Manhattan some years ago, as a former NYC-area resident. Although I think many people may run away from the rough winters, congestion, and high cost of living of that area, which is my subjective opinion of course, my old boss used to say that serious companies want to have a Manhattan address, almost like a "trophy" address for prestige.

For instance, his company maintained locations in Florida where work was done, but used a Manhattan office to entertain visiting clients and business partners, and that is where having world-class food/beverage options within walking distance comes into play, not to mention a quick ride to/from major international airports with several daily direct flights to key European business capitals. Thus, despite growth in cities in the south and west, NYC remains a key global financial and business center.

So, I think a bullish case is justified in the category of top-line growth potential, considering Vornado's expanding pipeline across office/retail/food and beverage, expansion beyond NYC to other areas, and a top-tier tenant list without being over-concentrated in a single tenant.

Low Margins And High OpEx Could Impact Bottom Line Growth, While EPS Estimate Negative This Year

Next, I will focus on what could impact the bottom-line, or earnings growth.

I often talk about the importance of strong margins within a sector, so investors may question why Vornado's net income margin is just 3.7%, according to Seeking Alpha data, compared to the sector average of 9.3%.

In addition, its net profit margin is in the middle of the peer group I selected, with ESRT having a much better margin:

I think this data is relevant for investors comparing different office/retail REITs and picking one that is most efficient at turning a profit, that they may want to add to their portfolio.

Considering the trends on the income statement for the quarter ending March, what we can expect going forward, if the trends continue, is that property expenses are a key cost in this type of business, and total OpEx eats up 82% of gross revenues. In some quarters, the firm may get a boost from selling an asset at a gain, and we saw such a gain in Q1, helping to achieve a YoY growth in net income, but those are not each quarter, so there could be lopsided trends.

Rather than just telling, the following visual "shows" the impact to operating income from OpEx, leaving the firm with just $82MM in operating income for the quarter:

In terms of selling assets at a gain, it did so in Jan. 2025 (Q1) and according to its Q1 results commentary "The net proceeds were used to partially redeem Vornado’s preferred equity on the asset."

So, I am leaning less bullish in terms of bottom-line growth potential, due to the weaker margins, the large impact to operating income, and the sporadic nature of asset gains/losses impacting earnings.

Also, I would mention the consensus is calling for an EPS decline this year actually, with a return again the next few years, so I think that data may cause many investors and analysts to be hesitant in buying.

Future Balance Sheet Risk Impacted By High Debt/Equity, And New Borrowing Recently

Next, let's consider some factors impacting balance sheet risk.

For one, debt to equity is a key factor I track, as it gives me a sense of a firm's leverage in relation to its equity.

Compared to the 2 peers I picked, Vornado has the highest D/E ratio of all, and has actually gone up in this 3 year period showing:

From the angle of credit rating agencies, according to the company website, 3 major agencies gave this firm ratings that are sort of on the border of investment and non-investment grade:

In the most recent rating note I found from Fitch, for instance, which was back in Aug. 2024, they seemed to share my concern about rising leverage risk at this firm. In their rating note, they said:

Fitch expects that (re)development costs in the next two years on the Penn District projects, combined with projected near-term occupancy headwinds, will cause VNO's leverage to increase to around the mid-8x level in 2024 before coming down to the mid-7x level in 2025.

Although the firm repaid $450MM in senior unsecured notes in January, according to Q1 results, one of their joint ventures took on $450MM in new financing as follows:

On April 14, 2025, the Fifth Avenue and Times Square JV completed a $450,000,000 financing of 1535 Broadway. The interest only non-recourse loan bears interest at a fixed rate of 6.90% and matures in May 2030.

Thus, investors should think about how the role of interest expenses on such loans can also impact earnings during this time, and then the future debt maturity payment.

Due to the credit ratings being not exactly investment-grade but close, and a higher D/E vs two similar peers, I am more cautious than bullish in the category of balance sheet risk.

Hoping For More Positive Dividend Growth Trends in Future

With dividend payments being a key benefit of owning shares in REITs, as I've said in many articles, particularly since IRS rules require that 90% of a REIT's earnings be paid back to shareholders via dividends, in this section let's focus on a dividend topic of interest.

What really got my attention and not in a good way was that Seeking Alpha gave this stock an "F" grade in terms of 3/5/10yr dividend growth CAGR. For example, its 10yr dividend CAGR is -10%, though the sector average is +2%.

Though its two peers, SLG and ESRT, also had negative CAGR, it was not as bad as Vornado.

Consider the following trends I highlighted below in the dividend growth chart, showing a decline since 2019:

I think this may be an issue of concern to many investors and other analysts, but that is just my opinion, since some investors may not care about dividends at all.

Of those that do, I think we should consider what could drive future growth of dividends and not just historic results, and I think it can be strong operating cashflow, because dividends are paid from cashflow. Unfortunately, Seeking Alpha data shows an expected fwd operating cashflow growth of -17%, even though the sector avg is nearly +5%.

For these reasons, I am not bullish when it comes to dividend growth potential.

Valuation Driving Positive 3-Year Forecast on Share Price

In this final rating category, I hope to merge the discussion of valuation and price targets as it has worked out well in recent articles I published, and showing the relationship between the two.

This being a REIT, the valuation metric of choice, and a forward-looking one, would be the fwd P/FFO (price to funds from operations). In this case, it is a multiple of 16.94x, vs the sector average of 12.67.

I believe the market is reacting bullishly after the firm's Q1 results which show YoY growth in top-line revenue, net income, and operating income, as seen on the income statement too.

So, as of this article writing, this market momentum has driven the shares up to around their 200-day moving average:

As far as they could continue climbing up past that average, I created the following price targets table (using FFO consensus estimates x fwd P/FFO = price target):

From my forecast above, for this fiscal year there could be some downside however in the next two years I expect share price to grow, with a 3 year avg gain of 5%.

This presents a modest case for bullishness, then, in the category of share price upside potential.

I like to compare, however, my price target with that of at least one major financial firm. Weeks before Vornado's Q1 results came out, it seems Piper Sandler (PIPR) lowered the price target to $38, and also mentioned "analysts projecting a challenging year ahead for net income."

Piper's price target is somewhere in between my year 1 and year 2 price target ($36.93 - $40.49), so I am not too far off the mark from a major financial firm's target then.

Conclusion: Agree With Seeking Alpha Quant System Rating, Hold

To conclude, my neutral sentiment today agrees with the Seeking Alpha quant system, which gave a Hold rating to this stock.

In this article, I showed why I think its best qualities are market demand and portfolio growth, as well as future price targets creating upside. However, it shows weaker qualities in terms of margins, dividend growth potential, and balance sheet risk.

A challenge to my neutral rating, to the upside, could be that profit margins greatly improve at this REIT in the next few quarters, and the firm continues beating EPS estimates, making my outlook overly cautious.

A challenge on the downside, though, could be that a key tenant of its runs into financial difficulty or even bankruptcy, posing a risk to rental income and making my outlook overly optimistic. However, what offsets this risk is portfolio diversification, as I mentioned earlier, and no overconcentration in a single tenant driving more than 8% of annual rent.

As I have many readers who are REIT investors, I welcome your commentary on what you think about the outlook for NYC and San Francisco real estate, and what else can be an impacting factor beyond what was said in this article?

Rating Methodology

Albert Anthony is The Analyst, providing commentary on the 2025 financial markets, uncovering fundamental elements from financial statements and ratios, company reports, and macro trends, with a goal of determining the likelihood of a stock's future performance and why.

Performance data quoted represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For more current performance information to the most recent month ended, please visit hartfordfunds.com.

Share Class Inception: A, I, R3, R4, R5, Y - 9/30/11; F - 2/28/17. Performance shown prior to the inception of a class reflects performance and operating expenses of another class(es) (excluding sales charges, if applicable). Had fees and expenses of a class been reflected for the periods prior to the inception of that class, performance would be different. Since inception (SI) performance is from 9/30/11. Performance and expenses for other share classes will vary. Additional information is in the prospectus. Only Class A assesses a sales charge.

Performance for periods of less than one year is not annualized.

Holdings and characteristics are subject to change. Percentages may be rounded.

Market Overview

US fixed-income markets generated positive total returns during the first quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index. Trade tensions and monetary-policy divergence drove market volatility and an increasingly clouded economic outlook. Most fixed-income sectors underperformed US Treasuries as spreads widened amid an expected deteriorating growth/inflation tradeoff.

Performance Summary

The Hartford Low Duration High Income Fund (I Share) underperformed the ICE BofA 1-3 year BB-B US Cash Pay High Yield Index during the fourth quarter.

The Fund’s out-of-benchmark allocations to bank loans and investment-grade corporates detracted from returns over the period.

The Fund’s underweight to and security selection within high-yield credit was a detractor from returns as the sector outperformed duration*-equivalent Treasuries.

The Fund’s out-of-benchmark allocations to the securitized sectors were contributors to performance over the period.

The Fund maintained an overweight to the belly of the yield curve over the period, which contributed to returns as yields fell.

Positioning & Outlook

We continue to be long in our risk posture. All-in yields are attractive and our team’s investment philosophy to emphasize income results in a procyclical tilt in portfolios. We are actively looking to increase our income advantage relative to benchmarks. Uncertainty remains on tariff implementation, resulting in a wide range of potential outcomes for the near-term growth and inflation outlooks. Corporate credit spreads have widened off of their tights, and we continue to find value in areas of high quality securitized. Spreads could remain volatile as macro uncertainty persists.

We remain positive on securitized. Residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) valuations are mixed as some subsectors have tightened more than others. In Commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), capital availability for commercial real estate (CRE) should continue to improve, though we are monitoring economic-growth impacts from tariffs as CRE is tied to the health of the economy. In collateralized loan obligations (CLO), loan fundamentals have been resilient amid higher earnings. Expect muted defaults, though slightly elevated when including distressed exchanges. CLO structures are strong.

Investment-grade credit metrics remain healthy, and ratings momentum continues very strong. Forward-looking earnings expectations have begun to roll over, and the negative growth effects of tariffs may accelerate this trend. Spreads are generally trading tight to historical medians and are not fully pricing in the elevated risk of a slowdown in growth and credit conditions over the next 6-12 months. Though, all-in corporate yields remain attractive.

At March-end, high-yield spreads were inside the historical median, though absolute yields were above average. We are cautious on fundamentals; we believe the distribution of macroeconomic outcomes has increased with the new tariffs and US administration policies.

We remain neutral on the bank-loan space. Loan-issuer fundamental trends are declining from a strong position. Revenue growth has slowed but remains positive, and margins and cash flows have been resilient.

Footnote

  1. Effective 3/1/24, the Fund (formerly known as the Hartford Floating Rate High Income Fund) changed its name, objective, principal investment strategy, portfolio managers and benchmark as well as reduced the Fund's contractual management fee.

1Expenses are from the Fund's most recent prospectus at the time of publication. Gross expenses do not reflect contractual expense reimbursement arrangements. Net expenses reflect such arrangements in instances when they reduce gross expenses. These arrangements remain in effect until 2/28/26 unless the Fund's Board of Directors approves an earlier termination. Without these arrangements, performance would have been lower.

* Other share classes may have different ratings. The Morningstar RatingTM for funds, or "star rating", is calculated for funds and separate accounts with at least a 3-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. Star rating based on a Morningstar Risk Adjusted Return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance (without adjusting for any sales load, if applicable), placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. 5 stars are assigned to the top 10%, 4 stars to the next 22.5%, 3 stars to the next 35%, 2 stars to the next 22.5%, and 1 star to the bottom 10%. Overall Morningstar Rating is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. For more information about the Morningstar Fund Ratings, including their methodology, please go to global.morningstar.com/managerdisclosures. ©2025 Morningstar, Inc. All rights reserved. The information contained herein: (1) is proprietary to Morningstar and/or its content providers; (2) may not be copied or distributed; and (3) is not warranted to be accurate, complete or timely. Neither Morningstar nor its content providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information.

Important Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Security prices fluctuate in value depending on general market and economic conditions and the prospects of individual companies. • Investments in high-yield ("junk") bonds involve greater risk of price volatility, illiquidity, and default than higher-rated debt securities. • The risks associated with mortgage-related and asset-backed securities as well as collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) include credit, interest-rate, prepayment, liquidity, default and extension risk. There are additional risks associated with credit risk transfer securities. • Loans can be difficult to value and less liquid than other types of debt instruments; they are also subject to nonpayment, collateral, bankruptcy, default, extension, prepayment and insolvency risks. • Fixed income security risks include credit, liquidity, call, duration, event and interest-rate risk. As interest rates rise, bond prices generally fall. • Derivatives are generally more volatile and sensitive to changes in market or economic conditions than other securities; their risks include currency, leverage, liquidity, index, pricing, valuation, and counterparty risk. • Foreign investments may be more volatile and less liquid than U.S. investments and are subject to the risk of currency fluctuations and adverse political, economic and regulatory developments. • Restricted securities may be more difficult to sell and price than other securities. • The Fund's investments may fluctuate in value over a short period of time. • The portfolio managers may allocate a portion of the Fund's assets to specialist portfolio managers, which may not work as intended. • Obligations of U.S. Government agencies are supported by varying degrees of credit but are generally not backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. Government. • The Fund may have high portfolio turnover, which could increase its transaction costs and an investor's tax liability.

Investors should carefully consider a fund's investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. This and other important information is contained in a fund's full prospectus and summary prospectus, which can be obtained by visiting hartfordfunds.com. Please read it carefully before investing.

Mutual funds are distributed by Hartford Funds Distributors, LLC (HFD), Member FINRA. Advisory services are provided by Hartford Funds Management Company, LLC (HFMC). Certain funds are sub-advised by Wellington Management Company LLP. HFMC and Wellington Management are SEC registered investment advisers. HFD and HFMC are not affiliated with any sub-adviser.

data.title: CVAR: Why You Should Avoid This High Fee Active All-Cap Value ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

With only a 12.15% total return since its launch on December 22, 2021, it's safe to say the actively managed Cultivar ETF (BATS:CVAR) has been a bust. Benchmarked against the Russell 3000 Index but designed to appeal to all-cap value investors, CVAR has also lagged behind most passive funds, and my analysis of its current fundamentals indicates that it's likely not to change moving forward. As a result, I've assigned a "sell" rating on CVAR, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

CVAR Overview

Strategy Discussion

CVAR is an actively managed fund that launched on December 22, 2021. As of May 5, 2025, it has 90 holdings, a 0.87% expense ratio, $31.50 million in assets under management, and is benchmarked against the Russell 3000 Index. As shown below, it's trailed that benchmark by 1.80% per year (4.40% vs. 6.20%) since its inception. Notably, the performance gap was 5.49% over the last three years as of March 31, 2025, though it has demonstrated solid gains in Q1.

CVAR's principal investment strategies, as described in the fund prospectus, note how the portfolio is comprised of "approximately 50 to 100 securities across a broad spectrum of market capitalizations and sectors." Furthermore, the strategy is value-oriented, which aligns with the categories assigned by third-party data providers like Morningstar. As shown below, CVAR's style has consistently been "Mid Value" since its inception, while its category changed from "US Fund Large Value" in 2021 to "US Fund Mid-Cap Value" thereafter.

To achieve its investment objective, the prospectus states the following:

The Fund invests primarily in equity securities traded on U.S. exchanges of any size that the Fund's investment adviser, Cultivar Capital, Inc. (the "Adviser") believes are under-valued or under-appreciated by other participants in the broader equity market. In selecting securities for the Fund, the Adviser screens on an ongoing basis the Fund's investment universe seeking such securities using an initial valuation assessment that is primarily quantitative, based on cash flow. The Adviser's discounted cash flow model uses several key inputs to assess each security's estimated intrinsic value. The key inputs include a conservative annual cash flow assumption, a desired rate of return, and an average growth rate for the security as implied by the market historically.

The prospectus also states that the Adviser aims for a 20-40% discount to their calculated intrinsic value and that common stocks, ADRs, and REITs are all eligible securities. Securities trading at sufficient discounts go through a "multi-layered review within the Adviser's portfolio management team," which involves assessing the security's relative risk and uncertainty of its future cash flows. Lastly, the Adviser gives consideration to "top-down, macro valuation factors, including sector diversification," so it sounds pretty comprehensive.

However, the strategy also hasn't worked well so far, and I'll try to shed some light on why in the fundamental analysis section. But first, let's take a closer look at CVAR's performance track record against the iShares Russell 3000 ETF (IWV) and three all-cap value funds: the WisdomTree U.S. Value Fund ETF (WTV), the iShares Focused Value Factor ETF (FOVL), and the Vanguard U.S. Value Factor ETF (VFVA). These ETFs have lower expense ratios than CVAR and are also more established, though WTV's strategy changed on December 18, 2017, so I caution against reading too much into its results prior to that date.

Performance Summary

Since its launch on December 22, 2021, CVAR has gained 12.15% compared to 22.19%, 38.43%, 32.94%, and 15.33% for IWV, WTV, FOVL, and VFVA, respectively. We knew from earlier that CVAR had trailed its benchmark, but it's disappointing to see it also lag behind other funds in the same category. It's also performed poorly compared to value ETFs, regardless of size. For example, its 7.11% three-year total return as of April 30, 2025, ranks #120/139 among all the U.S. value ETFs I track.

The next table from Portfolio Visualizer highlights some additional risk and return metrics for the five ETFs since January 2022. One positive feature is that CVAR seems to manage downside risk better than its peers, evidenced by a relatively low 17.07% maximum drawdown and -5.03% "worst year" figure, which occurred in 2022. Furthermore, the earlier table from Morningstar shows how CVAR is only 88.79% invested in equities, with the remaining in Treasury Bonds and Cash / Cash Equivalents. That's more than from 2021-2024, so it appears the firm's outlook for equities is diminishing.

CVAR Analysis

Sector Allocations and Top Ten Holdings

The next table highlights the sector allocation differences between CVAR, IWV, WTV, FOVL, and VFVA. Please note, these allocations are only for the equity portion of the portfolio.

We see CVAR underweights the Financials sector by 19.46% on average, though the number is smaller if you were to exclude FOVL, which allocates 55.41% to that sector. CVAR is heavy in Consumer Staples at 13.01%, which aligns well with the firm's seemingly cautious outlook. Still, the good sector diversification, as described in the fund's prospectus, is evident. Every sector is represented, and no sector exceeds 20% of the portfolio.

CVAR Fundamentals: Top 25 Holdings

Next, please consider the following fundamental analysis for CVAR's top 25 equity holdings, which comprise 54.41% of the portfolio on a cash-adjusted basis. I've also included summary statistics for IWV, WTV, FOVL, and VFVA at the bottom to help us identify CVAR's strengths and weaknesses.

Here are three observations to consider:

1. CVAR has a 0.85 five-year portfolio beta, which indicates it's less risky than the broader market. I calculated this beta using the weighted average betas of CVAR's current holdings, and as we see below, CVAR's one-year beta (based on the ETF's prior holdings) has consistently been below average, now at only 0.469. This tells me managers have been conservative for a while, and that's why they've missed out on the market's big run-up after 2022.

2. CVAR trades at 15.02x forward earnings and 10.34x trailing cash flow, which is quite elevated compared to the three value ETFs listed. In my view, WTV is a far superior option. Its forward P/E ratio is just 12.55x, it features better historical and estimated sales and earnings growth rates, and it's also less risky than the market, evidenced by its 0.96 five-year beta. FOVL is even cheaper, but that's because it's allocated over 55% to Financials, and I think that concentration in any sector is an added risk itself. As for VFVA, it's also cheap, but the growth isn't there, with analysts expecting just a 4.77% increase in earnings per share over the next twelve months.

One interesting statistic is that CVAR's holdings have seen their share prices decline by 4.38% over the last year, compared to +14.29% and +11.15% for WTV and FOVL. This could be caused by managers not adjusting their future cash flow assumptions when warranted, indicating they believe such price drops are only temporary. That could be the case, but what they might not factor in is that CVAR's holdings have negative earnings outlooks among Wall Street analysts. I calculated a 4.72/10 EPS Revision Score using Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, which ranks #168/190 among all the U.S. value ETFs I track. Furthermore, CVAR's holdings have done nothing over the last three and five years, declining by 23.24% and 9.99%, respectively. Intentional or not, managers essentially want to convince you that this is the moment they'll revert back to their fair value, which, I think, is a big stretch.

3. Another reason why CVAR's selections have performed poorly is because many are low quality. As shown in the first table, the portfolio's weighted average ROA and ROE figures are only 5.51% and 12.82%, respectively, compared to 8.02% and 22.01% for WTV, my preferred choice in this group. To account for sector composition differences between the five funds, I also calculated sector-adjusted profit scores, and CVAR's 7.76/10 is below WTV's, albeit better than FOVL and VFVA. In short, there are just too many reasons not to select WTV over CVAR, as WTV has:

  • a lower expense ratio (0.12% vs. 0.87%)
  • a stronger track record (38.40% vs. 12.15% since CVAR's inception)
  • better sector-adjusted scores on all factors except value (growth, momentum, quality, earnings revisions)

Investment Recommendation

I've assigned a "sell" rating to CVAR due to its poor track record, high expense ratio, and weak overall fundamentals. I was unable to assess the inputs of its proprietary cash-flow-based model, but the output is clearly contrarian, as the current portfolio consists primarily of stocks that have generated negative returns over the last one, three, and five years. I suspect the likely cause is a reluctance to adjust a company's cash flow assumptions as new information becomes available, which could include declining profit margins or negative consensus Wall Street earnings revisions. Either way, WTV looks superior, and I would suggest that as an alternative for all-cap value investors.

data.title: Hims & Hers: 2030 Guidance Indicates Big Upside
data.description:
Hims & Hers Health (NYSE:HIMS) has recently published their Q1 2025 earnings, which showed impressive growth metrics as well as ambitious guidance for 2030. When underlying the management's guidance, the company seems to be undervalued right now. But is this guidance and the resulting growth expectation reasonable?

To answer this, we will first analyse the published earnings, then evaluate what the new 2030 guidance from the management means for the stock price and eventually see how realistic this growth is.

Q1 2025 Financial Highlights

  • Revenue: $586.0 million - 111% increase YoY

  • Net Income: $49.5 million - 345% increase YoY

  • Subscribers: 2.4 million - 38% increase YoY

  • Monthly Revenue per Subscriber: $84 - 53% increase YoY

The company also reaffirmed their FY 2025 guidance of $2.3 to $2.4 billion and even improved their adjusted EBITDA margin expectation to ~13.5%.

Strategic Outlook

The company's management additionally gave guidance for 2030. They are planning with revenues of at least $6.5 billion and $1.3 billion in adjusted EBITDA, resulting in an EBITDA margin of 20%.

Hereby they focus on personalizing the healthcare service, expanding into new illnesses, follow-up care as well as new geographic markets, which should position the company to reach tens of millions of customers.

Market Reaction

Despite the strong metrics, the stock experienced a decline of ~6% after the earnings were released. Cowen, however, raised their stock price target to $38, meaning that some analysts are cautiously optimistic after the earnings.

I'm personally no huge fan of such medium to long-term guidance, as this can be used very easily by the management to pump the stock. Nevertheless, this gives us a very good opportunity to evaluate the company - at least when the guidance is realistic. But more on that later; Let's first look at the opportunities and risks that come with investing in Hims.

Opportunities

1. Further Expansion into Weight Management

Hims & Hers announced a collaboration with Novo Nordisk (NVO) in April 2025 through which they can offer the successful weight loss drug Wegovy on their platform. Hims members can as of now access Wegovy at a monthly price of $599.

With this deal, Hims is making obesity care more accessible for its customers and is also strategically shifting away from previously offering compounded alternatives. This aggressive push into the weight management sector is nicely complementing the core subscription model by addressing this high-demand segment.

2. Diversification of Services and Countries

Apart from the mentioned weight control medication, Hims is constantly extending its offerings, as they are now pushing into mental health care as well as chronic conditions, for example. Their collaborations with big retailers like Target (TGT) and Walmart (WMT) should also help to improve their customer base and the resilience of their business.

The company is currently only operating in the US. A potential expansion to other countries could therefore quickly lead to further growth.

Risks To Consider

1. Intensifying Competition

The Tele-Health market is very competitive, with competitors like Amazon's (AMZN) One Medical and Teladoc (TDOC) vying for market share. To keep or even expand their market share, Hims need to constantly innovate and improve their offerings.

2. High Risk - High Reward?

Despite the very high sales growth, Hims is only starting to get profitable. This leads to very high valuations on paper and high expectations by the market for growth as well as profitability improvements. Should the company fail to meet expectations, drastic declines and high volatility are imminent.

3. Insider Selling

Most insiders at Hims currently selling their stocks and almost no buys take place, which isn't exactly showing confidence in the company from the management.

Valuation

From the revenue and EBITDA guidance of 2025 and 2030, we can calculate the following revenue and EBITDA from 2025 to 2030:

Average Revenue Goal 2025: $2.35 billion

Revenue Goal 2030: $6.5 billion

Average Adj. EBITDA Margin Goal 2025: 13.5%

Adj. EBITDA Margin Goal 2030: 20%

I assumed the margin to improve by 1.5% yearly and the $6.5 billion revenue goal can be translated to a revenue CAGR of 22.6% starting from the 2025 guidance of $2.35 billion. After 2030, I anticipated the margin to stay constant at 20% and the growth to half to 11.3% per year.

For the Free Cash Flow Conversion I assumed that Hims manages to convert 90% of the adjusted EBITDA to Free Cash Flow, this is a bit lower than historical rates of 2023 and 2024 but still rather optimistic.

For the WACC, I assumed 10%, which is conservative compared to Hims current rate. I also believe that a Perpetuity Growth Rate of 3.5% seems reasonable for the company.

With these assumptions, we get a fair value market cap of $15.1 billion, indicating that the company could currently be undervalued by a whopping 62%.

Assuming that the profitability as well as the cash flow conversion stay the same as we predicted, Hims needs to achieve top-line growth rates of at least 16.2% p.a. until 2032 to justify the current valuation.

Assuming that the profitability, FCF conversion as well as the revenue guidance for 2025 stay constant to the first DCF, the required growth to justify the current valuation is even lowered to a very well achievable 10.4% p.a.

Is This Achievable?

Experts are currently predicting the following market growth rates for the global telehealth market:

Prediction p.a. Time Frame
Fortune Business Insight 22.9% 2025–2032
Grand View Research 24.7% 2025–2030
Precedence Research 22.5% 2024–2034

This gives us an average expected growth rate of 23.3% p.a., indicating that the company should be able to manage growth with the 16.2% p.a. needed to justify the current valuation. The guided growth rate of 22.6% until 2030 is also in line with this, further solidifying management's 2030 guidance and therefore our upside potential of up to 62%.

Conclusion

Within our Discounted Cash Flow Analyses, we get a required growth rate of 16.2% p.a. to justify the current valuation. If we, however, assume that the company manages to hit their 2030 revenue and EBITDA targets, Hims & Hers could even be undervalued by up to 62%.

When considering the expected underlying market growth rate of Hims & Hers business of 23.3% p.a., the company needs to only achieve ~70% of that to trade at fair value right now. This as well as the vast opportunities mentioned have prompted me to give the company a Buy rating.

I initiated a small position yesterday and will slowly build up this position trough dollar cost average as long as we are trading below $55.

What’s Your Perspective? Let’s Discuss!

Thank you for reading my analysis! I'd love to hear your ideas and points of view — whether you agree, disagree, or have a unique take on the stock. Let's start a discussion in the comments!

data.title: Philip Morris: A Bold Pivot That Worked
data.description:
Philip Morris (NYSE:PM) has been on a tear. Ignoring market turmoil, investors have pushed PM stock up 41% YTD. Underlying this rally is a fundamental shift within the company, which is successfully pulling off one of the most brazen corporate pivots in history.

The maker of Marlboro decided years ago to shed its cigarette stench. Quitting for good is never easy, any smoker will tell you, but the company is well on its way to reaching +50% in non-cigarette revenues and will reach +50% non-combustible gross margins sooner than that.

That day (or quarter) is getting awfully close, and it will mark a watershed moment: the premier tobacco giant, owner of the world's most recognizable cig brand, will have fully transformed itself into the main disruptor within the industry it used to lead.

PM’s non-combustible strategy

Leading the “pack” (pun intended), is IQOS. The heat-not-burn (HnB) brand has dominated all markets where it can be sold, with special success in Japan, a country known for its taste for tobacco. Already, IQOS has reached over 50% market share in most large cities there.

PM likes to point out the swiftness with which the IQOS brand reached $10Bln in sales, faster than other brands (Facebook, Tesla, etc.). The comparison is not as fair as PM started with unparalleled and global distribution capacity, but still impressive for a product that is regulated. IQOS sales have surpassed Marlboro (excl. USA), which no one in the industry had been able to do, ever.

Despite this, IQOS has faced intermittent growth concerns (sort of visible in the periodic plateaus above), where it has become unclear if the traction is slipping. So far, all bumps have been quickly surpassed and HnB category is still the tip of the spear in PM’s non-combustible strategy. As of 2024, PM has achieved $15B in smoke-free revenues, $11B of those come from IQOS.

To put those $15B into perspective, PM’s largest global tobacco peer is British American Tobacco. Also on their way to transformation (but lagging PM by almost all metrics), BTI had yearly revenues of $32B for all of 2024. That means that PM’s smoke-free business is already 47% the size of its closest tobacco industry peer.

By focusing on IQOS, PM side-stepped the vaping rush which has left the market completely saturated with illicit brands almost everywhere. Not only are illicit products difficult to stop, especially in international and emerging markets, their producers are innovating faster – perhaps not surprising, giving they do not bother to seek regulatory approvals; but still a little humbling for established players used to having regulatory barriers around their businesses. The barriers still exist, they are just full of holes that nobody is really patrolling.

The upside of missing the vaping mess was offset by also missing the nicotine pouch boom. Pouches go into the mouth and seem to enjoy some of the circumspection that Warren Buffett notes when people choose Wrigley’s vs. the cheaper knock-off right beside it. For whatever reason, the pouch market has remained less “contaminated” by illicit brands.

PM fixed their slow start on oral by paying up for Swedish Match. The maker of Zyn was a logical step for PM. For one, it gave them a re-entry point to the US nicotine market. Second, in every other country when smokers switch out of cigarettes, PM sees the revenue gain but also the loss on combustible volume. In the US, it is all gain (the loss is shared by Altria (MO) and BTI, mainly).

Swedish match accounted for $2B of sales for PM in 2024, and is the largest growing brand in nicotine. Not only that, but unit margins at Zyn are better than even Marlboro, a feat most (including yours truly) though unrealistic only a few years ago.

We now have a wide gap between non-combustible industry leader PM, and it’s runner-up BTI. How are investors reacting?

Are investors fully on-board?

The world’s major oil companies are (or were) undergoing a similar sort of pivot. All of them, even Exxon (the Philip Morris of barrels), had a CO2 strategy. BP, perhaps the most aggressive in going down the greener path, found little reward and has since retrenched significantly.

I am not equating oil to cigarettes. The cost/benefit equation for oil and all its uses is not the same as that for cigarettes, which clearly have a very high cost and no benefit for society. The same goes for nicotine, but we cannot put that genie back in the bottle, the best way forward is to present dramatically safer options for those who wish to consume it. The point is simply that corporate strategy needs to be validated by the owners (investors) in order to succeed.

So, will success in smoke-free lead to a meaningful multiple re-rating for tobacco companies? The question is crucial, as only such validation will finally convince tobacco executives still “hooked” to some degree on their old ways…

With Philip Morris pulling away in non-combustible revenues, and thus meaningfully differentiating itself, we have an early indication:

PM's lead on non-combustibles has translated into a consistent premium vis-à-vis slower to transform peers MO and BTI. This year, however, the multiple to forward earnings is approaching that of Coca-Cola (KO), which I include as a benchmark of sorts.

While the strength of the Marlboro brand has always justified a premium to BTI, and PM's global presence (operating in markets where stick volumes are not dropping), justified a premium to MO, the gap has widened significantly in the last 12 months. This coincides with IQOS becoming PM's top-selling brand and, of course, the return to the most valuable nicotine market with fast-growing Zyn.

To top it off, a constant issue at PM is their relatively low proportion of hard currency earnings (while they report in US$, they earned in everything but US$). The depreciating soft currencies are usually on investors minds and show up in every earnings report. Zyn and its success in the US is gradually shifting sales (and margin) mix in favor of the dollar, mitigating future FX variability on reported earnings. The impact is beginning to show on the latest quarterly results.

Q1 results:

Earnings increased almost 13% during Q1 vs prior-year despite currency headwinds. This shows the positive effect of USD earnings coming from Zyn, and the product's superior margin. The most effective hedge is eliminating the risk altogether, and that is what USD earnings are doing for PM in regard to FX volatility risk.

And margins have never looked better, $3.8B on $9.3B in revenues means 41% margins, which are beyond industry leading, they are also at an all-time high.

During the next few quarters, Zyn capacity constraints will be eliminated as production line expansions ramp up; allowing production to finally catch up with demand (after more than a year of shortages). As this happens, Zyn sales are sure to accelerate both in the US and abroad. Helping to keep those record margins for a while longer.

Liabilities overhang

Tobacco is still tobacco, and the liability burden from past damage to society will not go away anytime soon. In time, and using "the Swedish experience" as a reference, those liabilities will finally recede. Sweden achieved smoke-free status mainly by promoting pouches decades ago. Their acceptance was only gradual, but by creating such a long track record, the societal health benefits of switching are now very evident.

The challenge going forward is using such "real life controlled experiments" to convince regulators elsewhere to allow reduced-risk alternatives. There are still many countries with very large populations that do not allow non-combustibles, but the landscape is gradually shifting (and the more evidence accumulates, the easier it becomes for holdouts).

PM is still working on reducing their debt load. Increased for the Swedish Match acquisition, the cash was put to great use. This is allowing fast deleveraging, with management expecting to reach their target of around 2x EBITDA by 2026. After that, share repurchases should follow.

Take away

Philip Morris has long held the first mover advantage on smoke-free. Their dominance in HnB has justified a premium multiple vs. peers. Recently, the gap has widened, as PM continues to press their lead and has now added a second category defining product with Zyn (at least within the US).

We may be two or three years away from PM becoming a mostly combustible-free company. An amazing pivot coming from the owner of the world's most valuable combustible brand. Investors, for their part, are embracing this evolution and rewarding the company with multiples more akin to leading consumer brands; the clearest signal for others to step up their own efforts.

The settlement of litigation in Canada will remove most profits from all tobacco companies there. But it will not touch non-combustible profits. At different speeds, all regulators will eventually realize that the best path forward is phasing out of cigarettes - by not fighting nicotine.
